title: Fix error-as-truthy bugs in maybe_quote_bigint and is_raw_json
author: devdanzin
body:
Fixes #350 (part of #348).

## Changes

1. **`maybe_quote_bigint`**: `PyObject_RichCompareBool` returns -1 on error, which is truthy in C. Now checks each comparison for -1 before using as boolean. Previously, a custom `__ge__`/`__le__` raising an exception would abort on debug builds or silently produce wrong output on release builds.

2. **`is_raw_json`**: `PyObject_IsInstance` returns -1 on error. The ternary `? 1 : 0` mapped -1 to 0 (false). Now propagates -1, and the caller in `encoder_listencode_obj` breaks on error.

Found using [cext-review-toolkit](https://github.com/devdanzin/cext-review-toolkit).

comments.author: etrepum
comments.body: This one doesn't seem to pass its own test

comments.author: devdanzin
comments.body: Sorry, I hadn't tested in all supported versions. I think it should work now.

comments.author: devdanzin
comments.body:
While fixing this, I noticed a minor C/Python parity gap in which comparison operators `maybe_quote_bigint` uses:

- C: `PyObject_RichCompareBool(obj, max, Py_GE)` / `Py_LE` — calls `__ge__` / `__le__`
- Python: `(-1 << bits) < value < (1 << bits)` — calls `__lt__` / `__gt__`

For plain `int` the results are identical. But for an int subclass that overrides comparison operators inconsistently, the backends could produce different JSON output. For example, an int subclass overriding `__ge__` to return `False` would not be quoted by the C backend but would be quoted by the Python backend.

This is unlikely to matter in practice, but if you'd like it aligned I can open a separate issue. The simplest fix would be changing the Python code to match the C operators.

comments.author: etrepum
comments.body: I don't have strong opinions on aligning the behavior between the python and c implementations when it only happens in situations where the usage isn't correct. The important thing is just that it doesn't create a some kind of leak/segfault/etc.
